scenes.title: Introduction
scenes.narration: In this revision video we'll cover quadratic graphs, applications of quadratic functions, nature of roots using discriminant, and quadratic inequalities. Let's get started.

scenes.title: Quadratic Graphs: Three Forms
scenes.narration: Quadratic graphs appear in three forms. General form shows the y-intercept c. Factorized form reveals x-intercepts alpha and beta. Completed square form gives the turning point h comma k. Remember these when sketching or analyzing quadratics.

blocks.latex: \text{General Form: } y = ax^2 + bx + c \quad \text{Read } y\text{-intercept: } c
\text{Completed Square Form: } y = a(x - h)^2 + k \quad \text{Read turning point } (h, k)

scenes.title: Example: Completing the Square and Intersection
scenes.narration: Express minus x squared plus six x minus fourteen and three x squared plus twelve x plus thirteen in completed square form. We complete the square for each expression. The first gives minus of x minus three squared minus five, the second gives three times x plus two squared plus one. Since the minimum y-value one is greater than the maximum y-value minus five, the two curves do not intersect.

blocks.text: Since the y-coordinate of min point is > than y-coordinate of max point, the two graphs will not intersect.

scenes.title: Application: Maximum Height and Intercepts
scenes.narration: A stone's height is given by h equals minus a x squared plus x plus two over five. Complete the square to find the maximum point. Equating the k-value to two point four gives a equals one eighth and horizontal distance four meters. Solving the equation when h equals zero gives the horizontal distance when the stone hits the ground, eight point three eight meters. The constant two over five is the y-intercept, the initial height.

blocks.text: x = 8.38 m represents horizontal distance when the stone hits the ground

scenes.title: Nature of Roots: Discriminant Table
scenes.narration: The discriminant b squared minus four a c determines the number of roots. If positive, two distinct real roots. If zero, equal real roots meaning tangent to the curve. If negative, no real roots meaning no intersection.

blocks.text: Distinct and real roots: 2 solutions ⟹ b² − 4ac > 0
No real roots: No solutions (no intersection) ⟹ b² − 4ac < 0

scenes.title: Example: Discriminant for Real Roots
scenes.narration: Find the smallest positive integer p such that four times p x minus four equals x squared has real roots. Rearrange to x squared minus four p x plus sixteen equals zero. Set discriminant greater than or equal to zero: sixteen p squared minus sixty four greater than or equal to zero gives p squared greater than or equal to four. Factorizing yields p less than or equal to minus two or p greater than or equal to two. Smallest positive integer is p equals two.

blocks.text: Common mistake: p² ≥ 4 does NOT mean p ≥ ±2

scenes.title: Quadratic Inequalities
scenes.narration: For inequalities like x squared plus x minus two less than two times x minus one squared, expand and rearrange to standard form. Factorize and sketch the parabola to determine the solution set. For inequalities holding for all x, the discriminant must be negative and the parabola must open correctly. Part b requires discriminant less than zero and coefficient a positive, giving the range minus eight less than a less than zero.

scenes.title: Summary and Next Steps
scenes.narration: We've covered completing the square, maximum and minimum values, discriminant for nature of roots, and solving quadratic inequalities. Practice the worked examples and attempt the practice questions. Master these techniques for your O Level A Math exam. Good luck!

blocks.text: Completed square form reveals turning point
Practice the worked examples and attempt the practice questions on page 7
